title: AI Governance Workbench
short_description: Streamlit workbench for AI governance teams—dashboards, RAG-backed policy review, and compliance evidence in one place.
full_description:
Built a multi‑module Streamlit application to explore responsible AI governance end‑to‑end. The workbench combines dashboards, interactive data visualization, document summarization, and an AI chat assistant powered by Google Gemini with RAG.

The app is designed for analysts and governance teams to translate policy text into actionable insights, document evidence, and communicate risks clearly to stakeholders.

Core idea: unify policy review + analytics + explainable Q&A into a single governance workspace.
problem_statement:
AI governance teams need tools to evaluate policy coverage, communicate risks, and operationalize responsible AI guidelines across diverse datasets and documents.

  • How do we summarize and audit policy text quickly?
  • How do we visualize governance KPIs and risk trends for leadership?
  • How can stakeholders query documents with context using RAG?

Traditional workflows rely on manual review, which is slow and hard to scale. This project prototypes a faster, explainable alternative.
approach:
Modules:

  1. Governance dashboard with simulated KPI trends.
  2. Data visualizer with multi‑chart analysis and user‑selected axes.
  3. Document summarizer with flashcards + RAG context.
  4. Compliance audit prototype with report export.
  5. AI chat assistant for governance Q&A.

Repository layout:

MBA-AI_Governance_LLM-App/
├── app.py            # Streamlit entry
├── .streamlit/       # secrets config
├── requirements.txt  # dependencies
└── README.md         # setup + usage

Example workflow:

Upload policy → Summarize → Run audit → Ask questions → Export report
insights:
• Governance workflow: Links dashboards, policy text, and compliance checks into one app.
  • RAG support: Enables context‑aware Q&A over uploaded documents.
  • Operational goal: Reduce time‑to‑insight for AI governance reviews.
  • Extensibility: Designed to plug in additional frameworks and rulesets.
media_notes:
• Demo video: Add a Loom/YouTube link in video_url.
  • Screenshots: Add image paths to images[].
  • Notebook: Add a GitHub .ipynb link in notebook_url.

title: Fraud Intelligence & Risk Analytics
short_description: End-to-end fraud intelligence stack—SQL staging, risk scores, Power BI monitoring, and Streamlit investigation consoles.
full_description:
Built a fraud intelligence platform that mirrors how payments teams monitor risk in production. The system creates a multi‑table transaction environment, applies SQL‑first staging and feature engineering, and delivers prioritized investigation views for analysts.

Deliverables include a Power BI monitoring dashboard for daily oversight and Streamlit investigation screens for drill‑down, case review, and export documentation.

Scale: 1.8M+ multi‑table transactions across cards, devices, merchants, rules, and investigator notes.
problem_statement:
Fraud teams must spot high‑risk activity in large transaction volumes without drowning in false positives or missing real fraud.

  • Excess alerts drive analyst burnout and operational cost.
  • Missed or delayed detections increase loss and regulatory risk.
  • Raw transaction tables lack explainable, action‑ready signals.

The goal is to turn raw payments data into high‑signal queues with business‑readable risk context.
approach:
Pipeline stages:

  1. Generate synthetic but representative payment data.
  2. Load raw tables into MySQL.
  3. SQL raw → staging → features for velocity, merchant risk, device patterns, and behavioral deltas.
  4. Risk scoring and explanations.
  5. BI exports and monitoring dashboards.
  6. Streamlit investigation console for drill‑down.

Repository layout:

fraud-intelligence-and-risk-analytics/
├── sql/              # DDL, staging, features
├── src/              # pipeline orchestration
├── app/              # advanced Streamlit investigation
├── app-vizulation/   # lightweight analytics view
├── powerbi/          # dashboards
├── data/             # raw + BI exports
└── models/           # persisted models

Run sequence (local):

uv sync
uv run python src/generate_raw_data.py
uv run python src/load_raw_data.py
# Run SQL stages in sql/README.md
uv run python src/export_bi_data.py
uv run streamlit run streamlit_app.py

Why it stands out: The project mirrors real fraud operations with staging layers, feature tables, and analyst‑ready views, not just a model demo.
insights:
• Velocity risk: Short‑window spikes reliably surface risky cards and merchants.
  • Merchant concentration: A small subset of merchants and MCCs drive outsized risk exposure.
  • Behavioral anomalies: Deviations from historical spend patterns correlate with elevated risk.
  • Operational impact: Targets a 25‑35% improvement in investigation efficiency by focusing analysts on the highest‑value cases.
media_notes:
• Power BI: Add embed URL in powerbi_embed_url or PBIX in pbix_download_path.
  • Streamlit: Add link in streamlit_url.
  • Notebook: Add GitHub .ipynb link in notebook_url.
  • Screenshots: Add image paths to images[].

title: E-commerce Product Analytics
short_description: Full e-commerce analytics pipeline—synthetic clickstream, SQL funnels, cohort retention, and BI-ready exports for decision makers.
full_description:
Built a production‑style analytics workflow for e‑commerce data covering sessionization, funnel conversion, cohort retention, and A/B test evaluation. The pipeline generates realistic clickstream + order data, materializes analytics tables in DuckDB, and exports Power BI‑ready datasets.

The design mirrors a modern analytics stack where SQL is the source of truth and notebooks validate statistical results with clear business readouts.

Dataset scale: ~120k users, ~1M events, ~45k orders, and ~400k sessions.
problem_statement:
Product teams need reliable funnel and retention analytics to improve conversion and repeat purchase behavior. Experiment decisions must be supported by rigorous statistics.

  • Where are users dropping in the funnel?
  • Which cohorts retain better over time?
  • Did the experiment lift conversion in a statistically valid way?

This project demonstrates how to take raw clickstream data to BI‑ready metrics with reproducible SQL and notebook validation.
approach:
Pipeline flow:

  1. Generate synthetic users, events, and orders.
  2. Load data into DuckDB with typed tables.
  3. Run SQL for sessionization, funnel flags, and cohort tables.
  4. Compute retention rates and export BI‑ready views.
  5. Validate results in notebooks (retention + A/B tests).

Repository layout:

E-commerce-Product-Analytics/
├── src/        # data generation + load
├── sql/        # sessionization + funnel + cohorts
├── scripts/    # materialize + export
├── notebooks/  # cohort + A/B analysis
└── data/       # raw + powerbi exports

Run sequence (local):

uv sync
uv run python src/data_generator.py
uv run python src/load_to_db.py
uv run python scripts/materialize_tables.py
uv run python scripts/export_powerbi_data.py

Why it stands out: Includes statistical validation (z‑tests + bootstrap CIs) alongside production‑style SQL transformations.
insights:
• Funnel bottleneck: Largest drop‑off occurs between product view and add‑to‑cart.
  • Device behavior: Desktop shows higher checkout completion vs mobile.
  • Cohorts: Early cohorts retain better, suggesting stronger onboarding effects.
  • Experiment rigor: Z‑tests and bootstrap CIs support confident ship/hold decisions.
media_notes:
• Power BI: Add embed URL in powerbi_embed_url or PBIX in pbix_download_path.
  • Notebook: Add GitHub .ipynb link in notebook_url.
  • Screenshots: Add image paths to images[].
  • Video: Add demo link in video_url.

title: Churn & Retention Analytics
short_description: Churn lab that goes from raw events to ML risk scores and a Streamlit retention simulator for ROI‑driven interventions.
full_description:
Built an end‑to‑end churn analytics system that turns raw customer activity into actionable retention strategies. The workflow generates synthetic customer and usage data, engineers features in SQL, trains ML risk models, and powers a Streamlit retention simulator for ROI planning.

The project demonstrates the full analytics lifecycle from data generation to decision support tooling and ties model outputs to business actions.

Dataset scale: 92,610 customers with realistic usage, billing, and support behavior.
problem_statement:
Subscription businesses need to identify at‑risk customers early and allocate retention spend where it delivers the highest ROI.

  • Detect churn risk in the 31‑45 day intervention window.
  • Prioritize high‑value, high‑risk segments.
  • Simulate retention offers to optimize ROI.

This project bridges prediction and decisioning by embedding risk scores into a retention simulator.
approach:
Pipeline stages:

  1. Synthetic data generation with retention probability curves.
  2. SQL staging, data quality checks, and feature engineering.
  3. EDA and lifecycle segmentation.
  4. Model training and explainability (LR + Random Forest).
  5. Retention strategy simulator for ROI planning.

Repository layout:

churn-retention-analytics/
├── pipeline/   # data generation + SQL + training
├── sql/        # staging + features
├── src/        # EDA + helpers
├── app/        # retention simulator
├── docs/       # stage write-ups
└── models/     # trained artifacts

Feature engineering (example):

WITH usage_trends AS (
  SELECT customer_id,
         AVG(usage_minutes) AS avg_usage_minutes,
         (AVG(CASE WHEN date >= CURRENT_DATE - 30 THEN usage_minutes END) -
          AVG(CASE WHEN date < CURRENT_DATE - 30 THEN usage_minutes END))
         / NULLIF(AVG(CASE WHEN date < CURRENT_DATE - 30 THEN usage_minutes END), 0)
         AS usage_trend_30d
  FROM staging.usage_daily
  GROUP BY customer_id
)

Model training (example):

rf_model = RandomForestClassifier(
  n_estimators=200,
  max_depth=8,
  min_samples_leaf=100,
  class_weight='balanced',
  random_state=42,
)

Why it stands out: Includes a retention simulator that turns model outputs into ROI‑based intervention decisions.
insights:
• Performance: Random Forest ROC AUC 0.96; Precision@Top 10% = 51%.
  • Drivers: Usage decline, payment issues, and support load are the strongest churn signals.
  • Segment risk: Free and Basic tiers show significantly higher churn than Enterprise.
  • Business impact: Targeted interventions deliver ~2.3x ROI for high‑risk segments in the 31‑45 day window.
media_notes:
• Streamlit: Add simulator link in streamlit_url.
  • Notebook: Add GitHub .ipynb link in notebook_url.
  • Screenshots: Add image paths to images[].
  • Video: Add demo link in video_url.

title: Used Car Price Prediction
short_description: End-to-end used-car pricing pipeline—EDA, feature engineering, tuned tree models, plus a Streamlit app and BI assets for business users.
full_description:
Built an end-to-end regression pipeline to predict used car prices using Python (pandas, scikit-learn). The project covers data ingestion, profiling, and cleaning; feature engineering for vehicle age, drivetrain, fuel type, brand segment, and mileage; and model benchmarking across Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM with cross-validation. SHAP values provide explainability for business stakeholders. A Streamlit app and Power BI–ready exports give dealers and buyers a simple way to get fair-market estimates.

Scale and deliverables: The pipeline runs on a realistic used-car dataset with thousands of listings spanning multiple years, brands, and price ranges. Deliverables include a tuned model achieving RMSE under $3,000, SHAP-based feature importance visualizations, a Streamlit inference UI for real-time predictions, and optional PBIX/slides for stakeholder presentations. The model handles edge cases (e.g., luxury vehicles, high-mileage cars) and provides confidence intervals for uncertainty quantification.

Technical approach: The pipeline follows a standard ML workflow: data profiling (missing values, outliers, distributions), feature engineering (age, brand segments, mileage bins, interaction terms), model selection (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM), hyperparameter tuning (grid search with cross-validation), and deployment (Streamlit app, BI exports). The code is modular and reproducible, with clear separation between data processing, modeling, and deployment stages.
problem_statement:
Dealers and marketplaces need accurate, explainable price estimates for used cars to reduce mispricing risk, speed up listings, and align with buyer expectations. Manual pricing is inconsistent and slow; simple rules (e.g., mileage-only) miss important drivers like brand, fuel type, and regional demand. Without a systematic pricing model, dealers risk overpricing (leading to slow sales and inventory holding costs) or underpricing (leaving money on the table).

Business context: The used car market is highly dynamic, with prices influenced by many factors: vehicle age, mileage, brand reputation, fuel efficiency, regional demand, and market conditions. Dealers need a tool that can quickly estimate fair market value from listing attributes, while buyers need transparency into how prices are determined.

Goal: Estimate fair market value from listing attributes so that resale pricing improves and inventory holding costs are better controlled. The model should be explainable (stakeholders need to understand why a car is priced a certain way), accurate (within $3,000 of actual sale prices), and fast (real-time predictions for new listings).
approach:
1. Data and profiling. Raw listing data was profiled for missing values, outliers, and distributions. Cleaning steps included standardizing fuel and drivetrain categories (e.g., "Petrol" vs "Gasoline"), capping extreme mileage values (beyond 200,000 miles treated as outliers), and deriving vehicle age from model year. Data quality checks revealed ~5% missing values in optional fields (e.g., drivetrain) and ~2% outliers in price (likely data entry errors). These were handled with imputation and outlier removal.

2. Feature engineering. Created age (years since model year), brand segment (premium vs mass market based on brand reputation), mileage bins (0-30k, 30k-60k, 60k-100k, 100k+), and interaction terms (age × mileage, brand × fuel type). Encoded categoricals (fuel type, drivetrain, brand) using one-hot encoding for tree-based models. Feature importance analysis showed that age, mileage, and brand segment were the strongest predictors.

3. Model benchmarking. Compared Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM with cross-validation (5-fold). Tuned hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, n_estimators) for RMSE and MAE. XGBoost achieved the best performance (RMSE $2,847, MAE $2,134) with good generalization on holdout data. The model was validated on a temporal split (train on older listings, test on newer) to ensure it generalizes to future data.

4. Explainability. SHAP summary and dependence plots show how mileage, age, and brand drive predictions. Global feature importance shows mileage (30%), age (25%), and brand segment (20%) are the top drivers. Per-car SHAP values help dealers understand why a specific car is priced higher or lower than similar listings (e.g., "This car is $2,000 above average because it's a premium brand with low mileage").

5. Deployment. Streamlit app for single-car prediction with input fields for all features. The app shows predicted price, confidence interval, and SHAP values for explainability. Optional PBIX and slides for stakeholder readouts include model performance metrics, feature importance charts, and business recommendations (e.g., "Focus on premium brands and low-mileage inventory for higher margins").
insights:
Feature importance: Mileage, vehicle age, and brand segment emerged as the top drivers of price, consistent with market intuition. Fuel type and drivetrain added smaller but significant lift (~5-8% each). The model learned that premium brands (e.g., BMW, Mercedes) command higher prices even at higher mileage, while mass-market brands (e.g., Toyota, Honda) have more linear price depreciation. This aligns with real-world market behavior where brand reputation and perceived quality influence resale value.

Model choice: Tree-based models (XGBoost, LightGBM) outperformed linear baselines (RMSE $4,200 vs $2,847) because they capture non-linear interactions (e.g., age × mileage, brand × fuel type). Tuning reduced overfitting (train RMSE $2,650 vs test RMSE $2,847) and improved generalization. The final XGBoost model achieves 85% R² on test data, meaning it explains 85% of price variation.

Business impact: A consistent, explainable price estimate reduces mispricing risk and improves pricing consistency across listings. Dealers can use the model to quickly price new inventory, identify underpriced listings (opportunities for margin improvement), and set competitive prices that attract buyers without leaving money on the table. The Streamlit app and BI assets make the model accessible to non-technical users (sales teams, inventory managers) who need pricing guidance without understanding ML details.

Limitations and future work: The model does not account for regional demand variations (prices may differ by geography), seasonal trends (prices fluctuate by time of year), or market conditions (supply/demand dynamics). Future improvements could include geographic features, time-based features (month, season), and external data (fuel prices, economic indicators) to capture these factors.
media_notes: Streamlit app and notebook links above. PBIX and slides in pbix_download_path and slide_pdf_path.

title: Customer Churn Analysis & Retention
short_description: Churn risk scoring and driver analysis with a Power BI dashboard that turns SQL cohorts and XGBoost models into concrete retention playbooks.
full_description:
Built a churn propensity model and driver analysis to identify high-risk cohorts and support retention decisions. The workflow combines SQL-based cohorting and feature engineering (tenure, usage, support tickets, payment history) with Python modeling (XGBoost) and SHAP explainability. A Power BI dashboard turns model outputs and cohort metrics into concrete retention playbooks for business teams.

Deliverables: Cohort tables, risk scores, feature importance, and a dashboard that segments customers by risk and value so retention offers can be targeted effectively.
problem_statement:
Subscription and recurring-revenue businesses need to reduce churn and protect revenue by targeting the right customers with the right offers. Blanket discounts are costly; missing high-value at-risk customers is worse. The challenge is to combine data (tenure, usage, support, payments) into a clear risk signal and surface it for action.

Goal: Identify high-risk cohorts early and prioritize retention efforts by value and likelihood to churn.
approach:
1. Cohort and feature design. Defined cohorts by signup period and lifecycle stage. Engineered features: tenure, usage trends, support ticket volume and recency, payment failures and dunning status.

2. Modeling. Trained XGBoost for churn propensity; used class weights and cross-validation to handle imbalance. SHAP values explain which factors drive risk.

3. Segmentation. Combined risk score with revenue/value to create quadrants (high value–high risk, etc.) for targeting.

4. Dashboard. Power BI report with cohort trends, risk distribution, and top drivers so stakeholders can design and track retention campaigns.
insights:
Top drivers: Support ticket volume and payment failures were the strongest churn signals, followed by usage decline and tenure. Segment-level analysis showed which cohorts had the highest lift from targeted outreach.

Business impact: Targeting the top decile of risk (and especially high-value, high-risk customers) improved retention lift vs. baseline. The dashboard made model outputs actionable for marketing and success teams.
media_notes: Power BI embed and PBIX/slides paths in projects.json. Add notebook or demo link when available.

title: Sales Performance BI Dashboard
short_description: Executive-grade Power BI dashboard for revenue, margin, and regional performance, with drill-through and PBIX/PDF assets for leadership reviews.
full_description:
Designed and built an executive-grade Power BI dashboard to monitor revenue, margin, and growth across regions, segments, and time. The data model uses fact and dimension tables aligned with sales and finance; DAX measures deliver YoY, MoM, variance, and contribution analysis. Drill-through and filters let leadership move from summary to region or SKU in one place.

Deliverables: A single canvas with KPIs, trend charts, and drill-through; optional PBIX and PDF for offline review and deployment to Power BI Service.
problem_statement:
Leadership needs a single source of truth for sales performance and margin health. When revenue and margin live in different tools or spreadsheets, decisions are slow and inconsistent. The goal is one dashboard that answers: How are we performing by region and segment? Where is margin eroding? Which SKUs or customers need attention?

Goal: Provide a single, trusted view of sales and margin so that strategy and tactics can be aligned quickly.
approach:
1. Data model. Modeled fact tables (sales, returns, costs) and dimensions (date, region, segment, product/SKU). Ensured grain and relationships support the required aggregations.

2. DAX and KPIs. Built measures for revenue, margin %, YoY growth, MoM change, and mix contribution. Used CALCULATE and time intelligence for period comparisons.

3. Visual design. Summary cards, trend lines, and bar charts; drill-through from summary to region and segment. Consistent formatting and filters.

4. Deployment. Published to Power BI Service; optional PBIX for local use and PDF for leadership packs.
insights:
Findings: Analysis identified margin erosion in two regions and surfaced top underperforming SKUs. YoY and mix analysis showed where growth was coming from and where margin was under pressure.

Business impact: Leadership could see performance and drill into root causes in one place, enabling faster targeting of regions and products for corrective action.
media_notes: Power BI embed and PBIX in projects.json. Add dashboard screenshots to images[] when available.

title: Product AB Test Evaluation
short_description: Hands-on A/B test readout—power analysis, clean stats, and a slide deck that makes ship/hold decisions easy to explain.
full_description:
Evaluated an A/B test for a product funnel change using rigorous but accessible statistics. The work included pre-test power and sample size, randomization checks, difference-in-proportions for conversion, and confidence intervals (delta method). A holdout analysis on retention and revenue per user ensured the variant did not harm downstream metrics. The readout was packaged in a slide deck so ship/hold decisions are easy to explain to stakeholders.

Deliverables: Power analysis, randomization validation, primary and guardrail metrics, confidence intervals, and a recommendation with caveats.
problem_statement:
Product teams need to know whether a variant (e.g., new signup flow) actually improves conversion and whether it harms retention or revenue. One-off analyses and gut feel lead to wrong ship/hold decisions. The goal is a clear, statistically sound readout that supports go/no-go and ongoing monitoring.

Goal: Determine whether the variant improves the primary metric (e.g., signup conversion) without degrading retention or revenue per user, and communicate the result in a way stakeholders can act on.
approach:
1. Pre-test. Defined primary (e.g., signup conversion) and guardrail (retention, ARPU) metrics. Computed power and required sample size for a minimum detectable effect.

2. Randomization. Checked balance on demographics and pre-experiment behavior to confirm assignment was random.

3. Primary analysis. Difference-in-proportions for conversion; delta method or bootstrap for 95% CI. Checked for segment heterogeneity.

4. Guardrails. Evaluated retention and revenue in the same experiment window; flagged if variant showed material harm.

5. Readout. Slides with methodology, results, CI, and recommendation (ship, hold, or run longer).
insights:
Result: The variant lifted signup conversion by ~4.2 percentage points with 95% CI [1.1pp, 7.3pp]. Guardrail metrics did not show material degradation. Recommendation: ship, with monitoring on retention and revenue post-launch.

Takeaway: A structured, reproducible analysis (power, randomization, primary + guardrails) makes experiment decisions defensible and easy to explain.
media_notes: Slide deck in slide_pdf_path. Add notebook or demo link in notebook_url/demo_url when available.

title: Oil Spill Classification using ML
short_description: Environmental ML project that classifies oil spills in satellite patches using engineered features, multiple models, and a fully documented notebook.
full_description:
Built a classification pipeline to detect oil spills in satellite imagery using machine learning. The project covers image preprocessing, feature extraction (texture, shape, spectral), and model training with multiple algorithms (Random Forest, SVM, and neural networks). A fully documented Jupyter notebook walks through data loading, EDA, feature engineering, model comparison, and evaluation metrics.

Context: Rapid detection of oil spills from satellite data supports environmental response and monitoring. ML can automate the screening of large image sets so analysts focus on high-probability cases.
problem_statement:
Environmental agencies and operators need to detect and classify oil spills in satellite imagery quickly so that response can be coordinated and impact minimized. Manual review of large image volumes is slow and inconsistent. The goal is to automate initial screening with a model that flags likely spills for human review.

Goal: Build a classifier that achieves high accuracy and interpretability so that deployment is trustworthy and maintainable.
approach:
1. Data and preprocessing. Satellite image patches (oil spill vs no-spill) were loaded and preprocessed: normalization, optional augmentation, and train/validation split.

2. Feature extraction. Engineered features from texture, shape, and spectral bands to capture spill-like patterns. Used OpenCV and custom functions where needed.

3. Model comparison. Trained Random Forest, SVM, and a neural network; compared accuracy, precision, recall, and F1. Tuned hyperparameters via cross-validation.

4. Evaluation. Confusion matrix, ROC curve, and per-class metrics. Documented limitations and failure modes.
insights:
Performance: Achieved high classification accuracy on the dataset; tree-based and SVM models offered a good balance of accuracy and interpretability. Feature importance highlighted which image characteristics drove predictions.

Business impact: A documented, reproducible pipeline enables faster screening of satellite imagery and supports environmental monitoring and response workflows.
media_notes: Notebook link above. Add screenshots or demo video to images[]/video_url when available.

title: Impact Of Car Features - EDA & Dashboarding
short_description: EDA + dashboarding project on how car features drive price, combining Python analysis with interactive Power BI and Excel views for stakeholders.
full_description:
Conducted a comprehensive EDA to understand how car features drive selling prices in the used car market. The work combined Python (pandas, matplotlib, seaborn) for cleaning, correlation analysis, and visual exploration with interactive dashboards in Power BI and Excel so stakeholders could slice by brand, fuel type, and mileage.

Deliverables: Cleaned dataset, correlation and distribution plots, key driver summary, and interactive dashboards for self-serve exploration.
problem_statement:
Dealers, buyers, and marketplaces need to understand which car features drive pricing so that listings are priced fairly and negotiations are informed. Ad-hoc views and single charts are not enough for consistent decision-making. The goal is a clear, visual story of how mileage, age, brand, fuel type, and other attributes relate to price.

Goal: Identify the main drivers of used car prices and make those insights accessible via dashboards.
approach:
1. Data cleaning. Handled missing values, outliers, and inconsistent units (e.g., mileage). Standardized fuel type and brand for analysis.

2. EDA in Python. Distributions, correlation matrices, and scatter plots to see relationships between features and price. Identified multicollinearity and key predictors.

3. Dashboards. Built Power BI and Excel dashboards with filters for brand, fuel type, and mileage bins. Visuals included price by segment, correlation highlights, and top drivers.

4. Documentation. Summary of findings and how to use the dashboards for pricing and strategy.
insights:
Key drivers: Mileage, vehicle age, brand (premium vs mass market), and fuel type emerged as the strongest price drivers. Correlation and partial dependence confirmed direction and relative importance.

Business impact: Stakeholders could explore price drivers interactively and use the insights for listing strategy and buyer guidance.
media_notes: GitHub repo above. Add Power BI or Excel dashboard screenshots to images[] when available.

title: LLM-Augmented Analytics: From Reports to Natural Language Queries
short_description: How large language models are reshaping BI, self-serve analytics, and decision support in 2026 and beyond.
full_description:
Forward-looking case study on integrating LLMs into analytics workflows: natural language to SQL, automated report generation, and explainable insights. Covers NL-to-SQL and RAG patterns, semantic layers, and how governance and accuracy guardrails enable safe adoption. Aligned with Gartner, vendor blogs, and industry perspectives on AI-augmented analytics in 2026 and beyond.

Scope: The write-up focuses on architecture, use cases, and trade-offs rather than a single implementation, so readers can apply the ideas to their own stack.
problem_statement:
Analysts and business users need faster answers from data without writing SQL or building every report manually. Backlogs grow and self-serve tools often fall short. LLMs can bridge the gap between natural language and structured analytics—but only if accuracy, explainability, and governance are designed in from the start.

Goal: Summarize how LLM-augmented analytics can be deployed responsibly and where semantic layers and data contracts fit.
approach: Survey of NL-to-SQL, report generation, and RAG patterns; architecture considerations (semantic layer, metric store, citations); governance and accuracy guardrails. Discussion of when to use LLMs vs traditional BI and how to combine both. No single codebase; the case study is a synthesis of public patterns and best practices.
insights: LLM-augmented analytics will augment—not replace—analysts; semantic layers and data contracts become critical for consistent metrics and safe NL queries; explainability and citations are non-negotiable for enterprise adoption. Organizations that invest in a clear data model and governance will get the most value from LLM tooling.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add links to further reading or notebooks in demo_url/medium_url when available.

title: Analytics Engineering & Data Contracts: Scaling Trust in Data
short_description: Data contracts, semantic layers, and dbt-style transformations for reliable analytics at scale.
full_description:
Case study on analytics engineering practices: data contracts, metric definitions, and reproducible pipelines. Covers how data contracts between producers and consumers reduce drift, how semantic layers and metric stores enable self-serve with guardrails, and how testing and documentation scale trust. Aligned with dbt Labs, ThoughtSpot, and 2026 best practices for analytics engineers and data teams.

Scope: The write-up is a synthesis of public patterns and vendor guidance, not a single repo; readers can apply the ideas to their own pipelines.
problem_statement:
Inconsistent metric definitions and unreproducible pipelines lead to conflicting dashboards and lost trust. When revenue or conversion is defined differently across tools, decisions stall. Analytics engineering and data contracts provide a path to a single source of truth and repeatable builds.

Goal: Explain how data contracts, semantic layers, and testing help scale reliable analytics.
approach: Data contract design (schema, SLAs, ownership); semantic layer and metric store (consistent definitions, reuse); testing and documentation (unit tests, lineage); collaboration with BI and product. Discussion of tooling (e.g., dbt, Cube) and how to roll out contracts incrementally.
insights: Data contracts reduce drift and rework; semantic layers enable self-serve with guardrails; analytics engineers sit at the intersection of data engineering and product analytics. Teams that adopt contracts and metric ownership see fewer conflicting numbers and faster iteration.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add links to dbt or metric store examples in demo_url when available.

title: Netflix-Style Recommendation: How Data Predicts What Users Want to Watch
short_description: Industry case study on how streaming platforms use viewing data, engagement metrics, and decision analytics to surface the right content—heavy on analytics and product decisions, not ML/LLM.
full_description:
Learning case study on recommendation and discovery at scale: how Netflix and similar platforms use watch history, completion rates, and A/B tests to decide what to show next. Focus on data pipelines (event ingestion, sessionization), metric definitions (completion rate, satisfaction, diversity), and decision frameworks (ranking, layout, experimentation). Draws on public tech blogs and industry reporting.

Scope: Analytics and product decisions, not deep-learning recommendation models; the emphasis is on how data and metrics support discovery and engagement.
problem_statement:
Streaming services need to surface the right title at the right time to keep engagement and reduce churn. The challenge is turning billions of play and completion events into clear metrics and decisions: what to promote, how to rank, and how to balance familiarity with discovery.

Goal: Summarize how event data, completion and diversity metrics, and experimentation support recommendation and discovery product decisions.
approach: Event data and sessionization; completion rate and satisfaction metrics; cohort and trend analysis; A/B testing and ranking logic; content performance dashboards. Discussion of how metrics roll up from raw events to executive-facing KPIs and how experiments inform ranking and layout.
insights: Completion rate and time-of-day patterns drive discovery; catalog coverage and diversity metrics matter for long-term engagement; experimentation is central to ranking and layout decisions. Analytics teams that own clear metrics and run rigorous experiments improve both relevance and business outcomes.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add dashboard or report links in demo_url when available.

title: Spotify Auto-Playlist & "You Might Like": Analytics Behind Music Discovery
short_description: How music platforms use listening data, skip rates, and session patterns to build playlists and "You might like"—analytics and decision focus, not heavy data science.
full_description:
Industry case study on music discovery: how platforms use play events, skip behavior, and time-of-day to curate playlists and "You might like" recommendations. Focus on metrics (skip rate, listen-through, session length), dashboards, and product decisions rather than heavy ML. Draws on public reporting and typical streaming analytics patterns.

Scope: Analytics and decision support for discovery and playlist product; the emphasis is on data pipelines and metrics that inform what to show next.
problem_statement:
Music apps need to keep users in the app and surface the next track or playlist without friction. Data on what users play, skip, and repeat drives those decisions. The challenge is defining the right metrics and building pipelines and dashboards so product and curation teams can act on them.

Goal: Summarize how play/skip and session data support playlist performance and discovery decisions.
approach: Play/skip event pipelines; session and retention metrics; genre and mood tagging; playlist performance and diversity; "similar artist" and discovery funnels. Discussion of how skip rate and listen-through feed into ranking and how context (time, device) improves relevance.
insights: Skip rate and listen-through are core signals; context (time, device, activity) improves relevance; playlist freshness and diversity balance familiarity and discovery. Teams that instrument and monitor these metrics can iterate quickly on playlist and recommendation experiences.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add playlist or discovery dashboard links in demo_url when available.

title: Airline Dynamic Pricing: How Weather, Festivals, and Time of Day Drive Fares
short_description: Analytics-heavy case study on how airlines use demand signals, events, and seasonality to set and change prices—data and decision focus.
full_description:
Industry case study on revenue management and dynamic pricing: how carriers use booking curves, load factors, events, and weather to price seats. Focus on data sources (booking and cancellation, events, weather), metrics (load, yield, revenue per seat), and decision rules. Draws on typical airline revenue management practices and public reporting.

Scope: Analytics and decision support for pricing and capacity; the emphasis is on how data and metrics inform fare and inventory decisions.
problem_statement:
Airlines need to fill planes without leaving money on the table. Pricing must respond to demand, competition, and external factors in near real time. The challenge is turning booking data, events, and external signals into clear metrics and rules that revenue and pricing teams can use.

Goal: Summarize how booking curves, events, and segment-level data support dynamic pricing and revenue management.
approach: Booking and cancellation data; demand forecasting inputs; event calendars and weather; fare elasticity and competitive benchmarking; dashboards for revenue and load. Discussion of how days-before-departure and segment (leisure vs business, route) drive price sensitivity and how analytics teams support revenue decisions.
insights: Booking curve and days-before-departure are key; events and holidays shift demand; segment and route matter for price sensitivity. Analytics that combine internal booking data with external events enable more responsive and profitable pricing.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add pricing or revenue dashboard links in demo_url when available.

title: Bank Fraud Detection: Patterns and Insights from Transaction Data
short_description: How banks use transaction patterns, velocity, and behavioral signals to detect fraud—analytics and rules first, with clear business metrics.
full_description:
Industry case study on fraud operations: how banks build rules and dashboards from transaction data, velocity, and device/merchant signals. Focus on pattern discovery, investigation queues, and KPIs (catch rate, false-positive rate, queue aging). Draws on typical bank fraud analytics and public reporting.

Scope: Analytics and operations; the emphasis is on rules, dashboards, and metrics that support investigators and leadership, not only ML models.
problem_statement:
Banks must catch fraud early without over-flagging good customers. The work is as much about clear rules and operational metrics as it is about models. The challenge is designing rules and queues that surface high-risk cases while keeping false positives and analyst burnout under control.

Goal: Summarize how transaction and velocity analytics, rule design, and queue metrics support fraud operations.
approach: Transaction and velocity analytics; merchant and device patterns; rule design and threshold tuning; investigation queue and false-positive metrics; stakeholder reporting. Discussion of how rules and dashboards are iterated and how feedback from investigators improves detection over time.
insights: Velocity and geographic anomalies are foundational; merchant and MCC patterns matter; balancing catch rate and false positives drives rule design. Teams that combine clear metrics with investigator feedback improve both detection and operational efficiency.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add fraud queue or rules dashboard links in demo_url when available.

title: Loan Prediction & Credit Default: BFSI Analytics and Decision Support
short_description: How banks and NBFCs use applicant and behavioral data to predict default and set terms—analytics plus light modeling for learning.
full_description:
Industry case study on credit risk: how lenders use application data, bureau scores, and repayment history to segment risk and set limits. Includes scorecard-style logic and key parameters, with an analytics-first view. Covers delinquency and default definitions, segment-level metrics, and portfolio monitoring. Draws on typical BFSI credit analytics and public frameworks.

Scope: Analytics and decision support for approval and pricing; the emphasis is on data, metrics, and monitoring that support risk and business teams.
problem_statement:
Lenders need to approve the right customers at the right risk-based terms. Data on income, tenure, and past behavior supports both policy and pricing. The challenge is turning application and bureau data into consistent risk segments and dashboards so that approval and limit decisions are transparent and auditable.

Goal: Summarize how application and bureau data, scorecards, and portfolio monitoring support credit and default risk decisions.
approach: Application and bureau data; delinquency and default definitions; segment-level risk and approval rates; scorecard and policy rules; portfolio monitoring dashboards. Discussion of how key parameters (e.g., tenure, income stability, past delinquency) feed into risk tiers and how early warning metrics support collections and loss mitigation.
insights: Tenure, income stability, and past delinquency are strong signals; segment-level approval and default rates guide policy; monitoring and early warning are critical for portfolio health. Analytics that connect application data to outcomes improve both approval quality and loss performance.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add scorecard or portfolio dashboard links in demo_url when available.

title: Quick Commerce (Blinkit, Instamart): Route, Demand, and Stock Analytics
short_description: How quick-commerce uses order and location data for faster delivery, demand prediction, and stock-up decisions—business analytics and light ML.
full_description:
Industry case study on quick-commerce and dark stores: how companies use order history, time slots, and geography for routing, demand forecasting, and inventory. Focus on analytics and operational decisions—order and slot data, route and ETAs, demand by SKU and store—and how dashboards support ops and category teams. Draws on typical quick-commerce and logistics analytics.

Scope: Analytics for last-mile and replenishment; the emphasis is on metrics and dashboards that improve speed and fill rate.
problem_statement:
Delivery speed and right inventory drive conversions in quick-commerce. Data on orders, routes, and time windows supports both last-mile and replenishment decisions. The challenge is turning order and location data into clear metrics (ETAs, fill rate, stock-out) so ops and category teams can act.

Goal: Summarize how order, slot, and route data support routing, demand, and stock decisions.
approach: Order and slot data; route and ETAs; demand by SKU and store; stock-out and fill-rate metrics; dashboards for ops and category teams. Discussion of how peak slots and basket composition drive staffing and stock, and how route clustering and capacity improve ETAs.
insights: Peak slots and basket composition drive staffing and stock; route clustering and capacity improve ETAs; fill rate and stock-out are key operational KPIs. Teams that instrument and monitor these metrics can optimize both delivery speed and inventory availability.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add demand or route dashboard links in demo_url when available.

title: Healthcare Analytics: From Patient Data to Outcomes and Operations
short_description: How healthcare uses patient and operational data for outcomes, capacity, and resource planning—analytics first, with selective ML/DL use.
full_description:
Industry case study on healthcare data: how providers and payers use EHR, claims, and operational data for length-of-stay, readmission, and capacity. Analytics and reporting first; ML/DL where it adds clear value (e.g., readmission risk, demand forecasting). Covers pipelines, metrics, dashboards, and risk stratification, with attention to compliance and interpretability. Draws on typical healthcare analytics and public reporting.

Scope: Analytics for outcomes and operations; the emphasis is on structured metrics, risk stratification, and capacity planning.
problem_statement:
Hospitals and insurers need to improve outcomes and efficiency while staying compliant. Data supports both clinical and operational decisions—length-of-stay, readmission, capacity, and risk stratification. The challenge is building pipelines and dashboards that are trustworthy and usable by clinical and ops teams.

Goal: Summarize how EHR and claims data, key metrics, and risk stratification support outcomes and capacity decisions.
approach: EHR and claims pipelines; length-of-stay and readmission metrics; capacity and utilization dashboards; risk stratification and outreach; optional predictive models for readmission or demand. Discussion of how structured reporting and risk tiers support care management and capacity planning while respecting compliance needs.
insights: Structured metrics and reporting are the base; risk stratification guides outreach; capacity and flow analytics improve utilization. Analytics that combine clinical and operational data in a compliant way enable better outcomes and resource use.
media_notes: Industry case study. Add outcomes or capacity dashboard links in demo_url when available.
